title: Building a Form Builder in Laravel (Typeform-style)
subtitle: Forms as data-driven schemas, dynamic rendering, server-side validation generated from the schema, submission storage, conditional logic, and spam protection — the Laravel architecture behind a form builder.

sections.h2: How a form builder fits together

blocks.p: A form builder is a data-driven schema that generates UI, validation, and storage at runtime. The pressure: forms are defined by users (not developers), so fields are data; the same schema must render the form, validate submissions server-side, and shape how responses are stored and reported.

blocks.diagram:
User (form creator)
 └─ Form (schema = ordered fields as JSON/rows)
     ├─ Fields (type, label, required, options, rules, logic)
     └─ Submissions (answers keyed by field)
          └─ used for export, aggregation, webhooks
 Public respondent ──► fills + submits ──► validated against schema

blocks.p: The defining action — a submission — is validated against the schema, not hard-coded rules:

blocks.diagram:
Respondent submits answers
   │
   ▼ load form schema (fields + rules)
   ▼ build validation rules dynamically from schema
   ▼ validate ; on fail → errors keyed by field
   │ on pass
   ▼ store Submission (answers) ; fire webhooks/notifications (queued)
   ▼ spam checks (honeypot, rate limit, captcha)

ul: Schema model — fields as data (type, options, required, validation, conditional logic).
Dynamic validation — translate the schema into Laravel validation rules at request time.
Submission storage — JSON answers vs normalized, and how that affects reporting.
Logic & protection — conditional show/hide and spam defences.

blocks.callout: A form builder is a runtime interpreter of a user-defined schema: one schema drives rendering, validation, and storage. The hard part is generating trustworthy server-side validation from data.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: Each field is a record: type (text/email/select/checkbox/file/number/date), label, required, options (for choices), and validation rules. Store fields as ordered rows or a JSON array on the form. The application interprets this schema; it never hard-codes a specific form.

The critical security point: never trust the client. On submit, load the schema and build Laravel validation rules dynamically — e.g. a required email field becomes ['answers.fld_3' => 'required|email']. Validation must derive from the server-side schema, not from client-sent rules, or attackers bypass it.

Two patterns. JSON answers: one submissions row with an answers JSON keyed by field id — simple, flexible, but harder to aggregate/query per field. Normalized: an answers table (submission_id, field_id, value) — easier per-field reporting, more rows/joins. JSON is the common default; add normalization or a reporting projection when analytics demand it.

Forms change over time. Key answers by a stable field id (not label or position), so renaming a field or reordering doesn't orphan past responses. Consider versioning the schema so a submission records which schema version it answered.

Fields can show/hide based on earlier answers ('if country = US, show state'). Store logic as data (conditions referencing field ids). The client uses it for UX, but the server must re-evaluate it during validation — only require/accept fields that should be visible given the submitted answers.

File fields need validated mime/size, storage on a disk (S3), and the stored path/URL recorded in the answer. Stream large files, scan if needed, and use signed/temporary URLs so responses aren't publicly guessable.

Public forms attract bots. Layer defences: a honeypot field (hidden; if filled, drop), rate limiting per IP, optional CAPTCHA, and timing checks. Keep them server-side; client-only checks are trivially bypassed.

blocks.callout: Trusting client-sent validation rules (or validating only in the browser) is the classic form-builder vulnerability. Always rebuild and run validation from the server-side schema on every submission.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: A form builder stores forms as field schemas, interprets them at runtime to render UI and to generate server-side validation, then stores submissions keyed by stable field ids — with conditional logic re-evaluated and spam filtered on the server.

A form is a recipe card (the schema) that the kitchen (server) follows to both cook (render) and inspect the dish (validate). You'd never let a diner hand you their own inspection checklist (client-sent rules) — the kitchen keeps the authoritative recipe. Ingredients are labelled by a fixed code, not their position on the card, so reordering the recipe doesn't ruin past meals (stable field ids). Some steps only apply under conditions ('if vegetarian, skip the stock') — and the kitchen re-checks those conditions itself.

head: Storage
Flexibility
Per-field reporting
When
rows: Scale: write JSON, project for BI

blocks.diagram:
┌──────────────┐
           │  Form Schema │ (fields as data)
           └──────┬───────┘
        ┌─────────┼─────────┐
        ▼         ▼         ▼
     Render   Validate   Store/shape
      UI      (server)   submissions

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative.

blocks.h3: Lesson 2 — Build validation from the schema
Lesson 5 — Key answers by stable field id

blocks.code:
// answers: { "fld_8f3": "Alice", "fld_2a1": "US" }
// renaming the label or reordering does NOT change the key → past responses stay valid

blocks.h3: Lesson 7 — File answers store a path

items.q: Q1. Why are form fields modelled as data rather than code?
items.a: Forms are defined by users, not developers. Storing fields as records/JSON lets one application interpret any form — rendering, validating, and storing — without code changes per form.

items.q: Q2. Where must validation rules come from, and why?
items.a: From the server-side schema, rebuilt on each submission — never from client-sent rules or browser-only checks. Otherwise an attacker simply omits or alters the rules and bypasses validation.

items.q: Q3. Compare JSON vs normalized submission storage.
items.a: JSON answers (one row, keyed by field id) is simple and flexible but harder to aggregate per field. A normalized answers table eases per-field reporting at the cost of rows/joins. JSON is the default; add normalization/projection for analytics.

items.q: Q4. Why key answers by a stable field id instead of label or position?
items.a: So renaming a field or reordering the form doesn't orphan or misalign past responses. The id is the durable identity; labels and positions change.

items.q: Q5. How must conditional (show/hide) logic be handled on the server?
items.a: Re-evaluate it during validation: determine which fields should be visible given the submitted answers, and only require/accept those. Client-side logic is UX only and untrusted.

items.q: Q6. Name three server-side spam defences for a public form.
items.a: Honeypot field (drop if filled), per-IP rate limiting, and optional CAPTCHA/timing checks. All enforced server-side; client-only checks are trivially bypassed.

items.q: Q7. How are file uploads handled in answers?
items.a: Validate mime/size, store on a disk (e.g. S3), record the path in the answer, and serve later via temporary signed URLs so responses aren't publicly guessable.

blocks.h3: Code drill — server validation can't be bypassed (Pest)

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory; redraw 'schema drives everything' each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Flashcards; explain JSON vs normalized aloud.
Interleaved quiz; write rulesFor() from blank.
Teach server-side logic re-evaluation to someone.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. A pentester submits invalid data by editing the page and it saves. Root cause?
items.a: Validation was client-side or trusted client-sent rules. Rebuild validation from the server schema on every submission so the browser can't be the authority.

items.q: M2. After a form owner renamed a field, old responses show blanks for it. Why and fix?
items.a: Answers were keyed by label/position, which changed. Key answers by a stable field id (and version the schema) so renames/reorders don't orphan data.

items.q: M3. Analytics on a single question are painfully slow with JSON storage. Options?
items.a: Query JSON paths with an index/generated column, or maintain a normalized answers table / reporting projection populated on submit for per-field aggregation.

items.q: M4. A 'state' field is required even when the user picked a non-US country. Bug?
items.a: Conditional logic not re-evaluated server-side — the field should be hidden (and not required) given the answers. Compute visible fields from submitted answers before validating.

items.q: M5. Bots are flooding a public form. First, cheapest defences?
items.a: Add a honeypot field (drop if filled) and per-IP rate limiting; escalate to CAPTCHA/timing checks if needed. All server-side.

items.q: M6. When is a normalized answers table worth the extra complexity?
items.a: When per-field reporting/aggregation/filtering is a core feature at scale, where JSON-path queries become too slow. For most forms, JSON answers (optionally with a projection) are simpler and enough.

ul: Build validation two ways — trusting client rules vs rebuilding from schema — and exploit the first to feel why the second is mandatory.
Break field identity on purpose — key answers by label, rename a field, watch responses orphan.
Write the bypass tests first: missing required, hidden-field injection, honeypot tripped.
Add multi-page forms with partial saves and resume tokens.
Stretch goals: answer-piping/personalization, calculated fields, webhook + Zapier delivery, response analytics dashboard, and schema versioning per submission.

flashcards: What a form builder fundamentally is
A runtime interpreter of a user-defined field schema driving render, validation, and storage.
Why fields are data not code
Users define forms; one app interprets any schema without per-form code.
Where validation rules must come from
The server-side schema, rebuilt every submission — never client-sent rules or browser-only checks.
The classic security flaw
Trusting client validation; attackers omit/alter rules to bypass it.
JSON vs normalized submissions
JSON: simple/flexible, harder per-field reporting. Normalized: easy reporting, more joins. JSON is default.
Why key answers by stable field id
Renames/reorders don't orphan past responses; id is durable identity.
Server-side conditional logic
Re-evaluate visibility from submitted answers; only require/accept fields that should show.
Three spam defences
Honeypot field, per-IP rate limiting, CAPTCHA/timing — all server-side.
File answer handling
Validate mime/size, store on S3, save path, serve via temporary signed URLs.
Schema versioning
Record which schema version a submission answered so old data stays interpretable.
Honeypot mechanism
A hidden field bots fill; if filled, silently drop the submission.
Reporting projection pattern
Write JSON answers, project into a normalized/BI store for fast per-field analytics.
glossary: The set of fields (as data) defining a form.
A typed input definition: type, label, required, options, rules, logic.
Validation rules generated from the schema at request time.
A respondent's set of answers to a form.
Submission storage keyed by field id in one column.
A row-per-answer table for per-field reporting.
A durable key for an answer, independent of label/position.
Rules showing/hiding fields based on other answers.
A hidden field used to catch bots.
A temporary, authorized link to a stored file.
A snapshot of the form structure a submission answered.
